feed.title: Wasil Zafar
feed.subtitle: Technical articles on embedded systems, AI, software architecture, business strategy, philosophy, and life sciences.

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 12: Evolutionary Genomics
entry.summary: The ability to read and compare entire genomes has transformed evolutionary biology from a discipline of inference to one of direct observation. Comparative genomics allows us to quantify evolutionary relationships at the nucleotide level, identify functional elements preserved across hundreds of mi

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 11: Paleontology & Fossil Interpretation
entry.summary: The fossil record is evolution's physical archive — preserved remains and traces of organisms that once lived. While incomplete (most organisms decompose without a trace), the fossils we do have provide direct evidence of life's history spanning 3.5 billion years. Fossilization is extraordinarily ra

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 10: Mathematical & Theoretical Evolution
entry.summary: Fitness landscapes are one of the most powerful metaphors in evolutionary biology — a way to visualise how genotypes map to fitness and understand why evolution sometimes gets "stuck" at suboptimal solutions. Sewall Wright (1932) introduced the concept of a fitness landscape as a topographical map w

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 9: Behavioral & Social Evolution
entry.summary: Darwin himself called it "one special difficulty" — why would an organism sacrifice its own reproductive fitness to help another? If natural selection rewards individuals that leave the most offspring, how can behaviours that reduce personal reproduction ever evolve? The answer has reshaped our unde

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 8: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo)
entry.summary: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo) is the field that bridges two great biological disciplines: evolutionary biology (how species change over time) and developmental biology (how a single fertilised egg becomes a complex organism). Its central revelation is breathtaking: animals as differe

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 7: Mass Extinctions & Biodiversity
entry.summary: Life on Earth has endured at least five catastrophic mass extinctions — episodes where more than 75% of species disappeared in a geologically brief interval. These are not gradual fade-outs; they are evolutionary crises that reset the trajectory of life, clearing ecological space for new lineages to

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 6: Co-evolution & Symbiosis
entry.summary: No species exists in isolation. Every organism interacts with others — competitors, predators, prey, symbionts, parasites, and mutualists. These interactions are powerful engines of evolution, driving the diversification, adaptation, and extinction of species across the tree of life. Mutualism is an

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 5: Human Evolution & Migration
entry.summary: The story of human evolution stretches back over 65 million years to the earliest primates. Understanding our lineage requires tracing a complex, branching tree — not a single linear chain — through dozens of species, only one of which (us) survives today. Think of it as a dense bush rather than a l

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 4: Phylogenetics & Taxonomy
entry.summary: Phylogenetics is the science of reconstructing the evolutionary history of organisms — mapping the branching patterns of descent from common ancestors. The result is a phylogenetic tree (or phylogeny), a visual hypothesis of how species are related. "Tree thinking" is the ability to read, interpret,

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 3: Speciation & Adaptive Radiation
entry.summary: "What is a species?" seems like a simple question, yet biologists have debated it for over 200 years. The answer matters enormously — species are the fundamental units we count when measuring biodiversity, the units we protect with conservation laws, and the units whose origins we seek to explain. B

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 2: Genetics of Evolution
entry.summary: Evolution ultimately operates on DNA — the molecule that stores heritable information. While Darwin knew nothing of DNA (it was discovered structurally in 1953 by Watson and Crick, building on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography), understanding molecular biology is now essential for explaining

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 1: Darwin, Wallace & Natural Selection
entry.summary: The idea that species change over time did not spring fully formed from a single mind. Before Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, several thinkers laid crucial groundwork — from ancient Greek philosophers who speculated about change in nature, to 18th-century naturalists who c

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 28: Emerging ARMv9 & Future Directions
entry.summary: ARMv9.0 (2021) was the first major architectural version since ARMv8 in 2011. It mandated SVE2 for server-class cores, introduced the Realm Management Extension for confidential computing, and continued a decade-long trend of adding new feature flags instead of breaking instruction semantics. This f

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 20: Clinical Biochemistry & Diagnostics
entry.summary: Clinical biochemistry (also called clinical chemistry or chemical pathology) applies the principles of biochemistry to the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of disease . Every blood test you've ever had — glucose, cholesterol, liver enzymes, kidney function — is clinical biochemistry in action.

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 27: Security Research & Exploitation on ARM64
entry.summary: Security research on ARM64 blends intimate knowledge of the ISA with an understanding of OS memory layout, compiler mitigations, and hardware defences. This part covers the attack path from memory disclosure through ROP/JOP chain execution, examines kernel exploitation patterns specific to AArch64,

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 19: Molecular Basis of Disease
entry.summary: Every disease, at its most fundamental level, is a biochemical disturbance — a disrupted enzyme, a misfolded protein, a dysregulated signalling pathway, or an uncontrolled metabolic process. From the single amino acid change in sickle cell disease to the complex metabolic reprogramming of cancer, un

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 25: Cross-Compilation & Build Systems
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Cross-compiling for ARM64 on an x86 host seems straightforward until ABI mismatches, missing sysroots, and LLVM triple confusion bite you. This part covers correct toolchain selection for both bare-metal and

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 26: ARM in Real Systems — Android, RTOS & Bootloaders
entry.summary: Theory is validated in production. This part walks through five real deployment environments where ARM assembly knowledge is directly exercised: Android native code via the NDK, two embedded RTOSes (FreeRTOS and Zephyr), the U-Boot bootloader that brings up hundreds of millions of Linux boards, and

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 18: Tissue-Specific Metabolism Integration
entry.summary: The human body continuously transitions between metabolic states based on nutrient availability , hormonal signals , and energy demands . Understanding these transitions is the culmination of everything we've studied: glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, β-oxidation, ketogenesis, the TCA cycle, oxidative ph

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 24: Linkers, Loaders & Binary Format Internals
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Every ARM binary you run passed through a linker that stitched object files together, assigned addresses, and emitted relocations that the dynamic loader resolves at runtime. This part walks from raw ELF sect

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 17: Adipose Tissue & Whole-Body Energy Balance
entry.summary: Adipose tissue is far more than a passive storage depot for fat — it is a dynamic endocrine organ that regulates energy balance, glucose homeostasis, inflammation, and reproduction through secretion of hormones called adipokines . The human body contains several functionally distinct types of adipos

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 23: Debugging & Tooling Ecosystem
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . The ARM debugging ecosystem spans from QEMU software simulation up to hardware probes with instruction-level trace. This part covers the GDB remote serial protocol, OpenOCD configuration for JTAG and SWD adap

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 16: Immune System Biochemistry
entry.summary: The immune system is a biochemical defence network that distinguishes self from non-self and eliminates pathogens, damaged cells, and tumour cells. It operates through two complementary arms: innate immunity (rapid, non-specific, no memory) and adaptive immunity (slower, highly specific, immunologic

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 22: Virtualization Extensions
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . ARM's Virtualization Extensions give EL2 a hardware-enforced isolation boundary between the hypervisor and every guest OS. This part walks the full path from EL3 dropping into EL2, configuring trap controls,

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 15: Digestive System Biochemistry
entry.summary: The gastrointestinal (GI) tract is a remarkable 9-metre biochemical processing plant that converts complex food into absorbable nutrients. Think of it as an assembly line running in reverse — instead of building products, it systematically disassembles macromolecules into their monomeric building bl

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 21: ARM Microarchitecture Deep Dive
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Understanding what happens below the ISA boundary explains every counter-intuitive performance result from Part 18. This part maps the journey from instruction fetch and branch prediction down through registe

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 14: Endocrine System Biochemistry
entry.summary: Hormones are chemical messengers synthesised by endocrine glands and released into the bloodstream to act on distant target cells. Despite enormous structural diversity, all hormones fall into three chemical classes — peptide/protein , steroid , and amine — and understanding which class a hormone be

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 20: Building a Bare-Metal OS Kernel
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Building an OS kernel from scratch on ARM64 is the ultimate integration of everything in this series: boot sequence, exception vectors, memory management, and scheduling all collide in a few hundred lines of

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 13: Kidney Biochemistry & Acid–Base Control
entry.summary: The kidneys are remarkable organs — each weighing only ~150 g but receiving 20–25% of cardiac output (~1.2 L/min). They filter ~180 L of plasma daily yet produce only ~1.5 L of urine, meaning 99% of filtered fluid is reabsorbed. Think of the kidney as a precision recycling plant: it first casts a wi

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 19: Reverse Engineering & ARM Binary Analysis
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Reverse engineering flips the assembly workflow: instead of writing instructions, you read them to understand what a binary does. ARM's ubiquity across Android, iOS, and embedded firmware makes it the most im

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 12: Liver Biochemistry
entry.summary: The liver is the body's largest internal organ (~1.5 kg) and the metabolic command centre — receiving 25% of cardiac output despite representing only ~2% of body weight. Its unique dual blood supply (hepatic artery + portal vein) gives it first-pass access to nutrients absorbed from the gut and make

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 12: Regional Dissection Mastery
entry.summary: The upper limb is a masterpiece of evolutionary engineering — a structure that has traded weight-bearing stability for extraordinary dexterity and range of motion. From the freely mobile shoulder girdle to the precision grip of the hand, every anatomical detail reflects millions of years of adaptati

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 18: Performance Profiling & Micro-Optimization
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Writing fast assembly is half craft, half measurement. ARM's Performance Monitoring Unit gives you hardware-level insight into every stall, cache miss, and mispredicted branch. Combined with Linux perf and a

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 11: Heart & Muscle Biochemistry
entry.summary: The heart is the most metabolically active organ in the body. Beating roughly 100,000 times per day , it consumes approximately 6 kg of ATP daily — more than 20 times its own weight. Yet the heart stores only enough ATP for about 10 seconds of contraction, making continuous oxidative phosphorylation

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 11: Functional & Applied Anatomy
entry.summary: Biomechanics is where physics meets anatomy — the study of forces acting on and generated by the living body. Every time you lift a coffee cup, climb stairs, or turn your head, your musculoskeletal system is solving a complex engineering problem: how to move bones efficiently through space using mus

entry.title: Part 1: Why Neural Networks & Their History - Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Neural networks are the engine behind the most transformative technology of the 21st century. They power voice assistants, translate languages in real-time, generate photorealistic images, write code, and diagnose diseases from medical scans. But why did we need them in the first place? Consider spa

entry.title: Part 2: Building Blocks — Neurons, Weights & Activations - Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: The artificial neuron is the fundamental unit of every neural network. Inspired by biological neurons, it takes multiple inputs, multiplies each by a weight, sums the results, adds a bias, and passes the result through an activation function to produce an output. Think of it as a tiny decision-maker

entry.title: Part 3: How Neural Networks Learn | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Imagine tuning a radio with dozens of knobs. Each knob position affects the clarity of the signal. Your goal: find the exact combination that minimizes static and maximizes clarity. Neural network training works the same way — except instead of a few knobs, you have millions of “weights&

entry.title: Part 4: Build Your First Neural Network from Scratch | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: In Part 1 of this series, we proved that a single perceptron cannot solve the XOR (exclusive or) problem. A single neuron can only learn linearly separable patterns — and XOR is fundamentally not linearly separable. This limitation, famously highlighted by Minsky and Papert in 1969, nearly kil

entry.title: Part 5: Neural Network Architectures Overview | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Neural networks are not one-size-fits-all. Over the past four decades, researchers have designed specialized architectures to tackle fundamentally different types of data and problems. Each architecture introduces a specific inductive bias — a structural assumption about the data that allows t

entry.title: Part 6: CNNs Deep Dive - Convolutions from Scratch | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Fully connected networks treat every pixel as an independent feature. For a modest 28×28 grayscale image, that means 784 input neurons — and if we connect them to just 128 hidden neurons, we already have over 100,000 parameters in the first layer alone. Scale that to a 224×224 RGB

entry.title: Part 7: RNNs & LSTMs Deep Dive | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Feedforward neural networks (FNNs) process each input independently — they have no concept of order or context. When you feed an FNN a word, it has no idea what came before it. But language, time series, audio, and countless other real-world signals are inherently sequential: the meaning of &l

entry.title: Part 8: Autoencoders & GANs Deep Dive | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Unlike supervised learning where we have labels guiding our models, autoencoders learn entirely without labels. The goal is to discover a compressed representation — an encoding — that captures the essential features of the data. This is the foundation of generative modeling: if we can l

entry.title: Part 9: Transformers & Best Practices | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: In 2017, the landmark paper “Attention Is All You Need” by Vaswani et al. fundamentally changed deep learning. The key insight was radical: instead of processing sequences one step at a time (like RNNs and LSTMs), look at ALL positions simultaneously. This single architectural choice unl

entry.title: Deep Dive: GANs — Generative Adversarial Networks | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Imagine a counterfeiter who tries to produce fake currency that looks identical to real bills, and a detective whose job is to catch the fakes. As the counterfeiter gets better at forging, the detective must sharpen their skills to tell real from fake. Over time, this adversarial competition pushes

entry.title: Deep Dive: GPT-2 Mini — Building a Language Model | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: A language model is, at its core, a probability distribution over sequences of tokens. Given a sequence of preceding words (or tokens), a language model predicts what comes next. This is the fundamental task behind GPT-2, ChatGPT, and all modern large language models — they learn to predict the next

entry.title: Deep Dive: ResNet — Residual Networks from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Before ResNet was introduced in 2015, a perplexing phenomenon haunted the deep learning community: deeper networks performed worse than shallower ones . This wasn't simply overfitting — even the training error was higher for deeper models. A 56-layer plain convolutional network consistently underper

entry.title: Deep Dive: U-Net — Image Segmentation from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Image segmentation is the task of assigning a class label to every single pixel in an image. Unlike classification (which outputs one label for the whole image) or object detection (which draws bounding boxes), segmentation produces a pixel-perfect map that outlines every object's exact boundary. Th

entry.title: Deep Dive: YOLO — Real-Time Object Detection | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Before diving into YOLO, it's important to understand where object detection sits in the broader computer vision hierarchy. There are three progressively harder tasks that a neural network can perform on an image: Object detection is dramatically harder than classification because the network must s

entry.title: Part 1: Tensors, Autograd & GPU Fundamentals — PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: PyTorch is an open-source deep learning framework developed by Meta AI (formerly Facebook AI Research). Since its release in 2016, it has become the dominant framework in research and is rapidly gaining ground in production environments. If you're learning deep learning today, PyTorch is the best pl
